nativeRange.habitat: Mangrove forests, coastal swamps, tidal pools, brackish ditches, and rotting logs; tolerates hypoxic microhabitats and survives extended periods out of water inside moist decaying wood

decorPreferences: mangrove prop roots or driftwood branches
dense surface planting (floating plants, water sprite)
moist hiding areas such as PVC tubes or cork bark above waterline

diet.notes: Feeds on small live and frozen invertebrates: micro-worms, white worms, baby brine shrimp, Daphnia, grindal worms, and small insects. Will accept high-quality micro-pellets and crushed flake once settled. In the wild forages on mosquito larvae, amphipods, small invertebrates, and detritus-dwelling organisms in mangrove pools.

goodWith: own species in species-only setups (one specimen per tank recommended)
small peaceful nano fish in larger tanks with visual barriers
avoidWith: fast competing feeders that outcompete for food

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase specimen photo of Kryptolebias marmoratus; standard reference image.

summary: The Mangrove Killifish is one of the only self-fertilizing vertebrates on Earth — a simultaneous hermaphrodite capable of producing clonal offspring. Native to Caribbean and Floridian mangroves, it is amphibious, euryhaline (0–100 ppt in the wild), and an iconic model organism in evolutionary and developmental biology.
careNotes: Kryptolebias marmoratus is a fascinating but specialist fish suited to experienced nano-fish keepers with an interest in unusual biology. It is one of the only known self-fertilizing vertebrates — the vast majority of individuals are simultaneous hermaphrodites producing internally self-fertilized eggs; true males exist but are rare in wild populations. The species is famously amphibious: wild fish routinely leave water during dry seasons and overwinter inside moist, decaying logs for weeks to months, breathing through the skin. In captivity a tightly sealed lid is essential, as individuals can and will climb damp glass. The species is euryhaline to an extreme degree — wild populations survive from near-freshwater rice paddies to full-strength marine tidal pools (0–100 ppt); aquarium keeping is most successful in slightly brackish water (SG 1.002–1.008) though pure freshwater is tolerated. Water changes should be performed carefully to avoid salinity shock. Mangrove biotope décor (prop-root tangles, leaf litter, floating plants) reduces stress. Best maintained as isolated individuals or very small groups with heavy cover, as mild conspecific aggression may occur in confined spaces. The species was reclassified from Rivulus to Kryptolebias by Costa in 2004; it is still commonly sold and referenced under the old name Rivulus marmoratus. Due to its scientific prominence as a research model, some specimens in trade may have laboratory provenance.
breedingNotes: One of the most unusual reproductive systems among vertebrates. Most individuals are functional simultaneous hermaphrodites capable of self-fertilization ("selfing"), producing genetically near-identical clonal offspring without a partner. Outcrossing with rare males also occurs, producing genetically diverse offspring. Eggs are deposited in moist substrate or on surfaces just at or above the waterline. Incubation is approximately 14–20 days at 25°C. Fry are miniature versions of adults and will accept micro-worms and baby brine shrimp immediately. Selfing individuals can be maintained alone and will produce offspring, making captive breeding achievable without a second specimen. Diapause (arrested embryo development) occurs in some eggs under dry conditions, a trait shared with annual killifish.

primary.name: FishBase — Kryptolebias marmoratus
primary.notes: Primary reference. Provided scientific name, taxonomy (Rivulidae, Cyprinodontiformes), adult size (to 7.5 cm TL), native range (Florida to Brazil, Caribbean), habitat (mangrove swamps, tidal pools, rotten logs), temperature range (20–30°C), diet (small invertebrates), and confirmation of euryhaline and amphibious biology. Reclassification from Rivulus to Kryptolebias (Costa 2004) confirmed.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Kryptolebias marmoratus
additional.notes: Detailed husbandry, water parameters (pH 6.5–8.5, temperature 20–30°C), tank requirements, biotope notes, and confirmation of amphibious and euryhaline biology. Noted that slightly brackish water (low-end SG) is beneficial in captivity.

additional.name: Tatarenkov A. et al. — Multiple paternity and self-fertilization in natural populations of Kryptolebias marmoratus (Proceedings of the Royal Society B)
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed source confirming selfing hermaphrodite reproductive biology, clonal lineage structure, and occurrence of rare males in natural populations. Foundational reference for the species’ unique reproductive mode.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Mangrove Killifish profile
additional.notes: Hobbyist-oriented husbandry guide; confirmed aquarium care requirements, feeding recommendations (micro-worms, BBS, small invertebrates), escape risk (tight lid essential), and recommended slightly brackish maintenance for optimal health.

additional.name: Moog O. et al. / IUCN — Kryptolebias marmoratus conservation status and range
additional.notes: IUCN Red List entry confirming native range (southeastern USA, Caribbean, Central America to Brazil), habitat (mangroves, coastal swamps), and listing status. Cross-checked country list and regional range data.

fish.breedingNotes: Self-fertilizing hermaphrodite — a single individual can produce offspring without a partner. Eggs deposited in moist substrate near waterline; incubation ~14–20 days at 25°C. Fry accept micro-worms and baby brine shrimp immediately. Some eggs undergo diapause under dry conditions.
